text: The pre-marked ostomy site is visible in the right lower quadrant of the abdomen.
The surgeon makes a small incision and uses a Veress needle to insufflate the abdomen, preparing for laparoscopic access.
Several trocars are inserted into the insufflated abdomen in a standard laparoscopic fashion, providing access points for surgical instruments.
The surgeon explores the abdomen laparoscopically and begins dissection by taking the vessels of the diseased sigmoid colon in a medial approach.
The surgeon creates a mesenteric window and proceeds distally towards the recto sigmoid junction, preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic stapler to transect the colon, leaving a visible rectal stump behind after confirming sufficient distal dissection.
The surgeon progresses along the descending colon towards the splenic flexure, continuing the dissection in the laparoscopic field.
The surgeon detaches the lateral attachments of the sigmoid colon using a combination of energy devices and cutting instruments.
The surgeon uses an energy device, such as a ligature, to take the mesentery, visibly separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon opens the lesser sac and proceeds to dissect along the transverse colon from proximal to distal, exposing the anatomical structures within the surgical field.
During the dissection, the stomach is visible to the left, the pancreas is located below, and the spleen is positioned ahead in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a combination of blunt dissection and an energy device to separate tissue layers along the descending colon, progressing towards the splenic flexure.
The surgeon approaches the splenocolic ligament, aiming to connect with the prior dissection and complete the mobilization of the splenic flexure.
Following the mobilization of the splenic flexure, the surgeon shifts focus to the hepatic flexure, preparing to continue dissection in this area.
The distal portion of the stomach is visible to the right, with the gallbladder positioned ahead in the surgical field.
The surgeon focuses on the cecum and uses a ligature device to prepare for taking the ileocolic pedicle, with the device visibly in use.
The surgeon observes a pulsating vessel located just ahead of the ligature device, indicating its proximity to the surgical instrument.
Enlarged lymph nodes are visible around the ileocolic pedicle, indicative of inflammatory bowel disease.
The surgeon carefully dissects through the mesentery of the right colon, ensuring clear identification and avoidance of the duodenum and the right ureter.
The surgeon transects the remaining lateral attachments of the right colon, completing the separation from adjacent structures.
The surgeon focuses on the transverse colonic mesentery, preparing to address the remaining attachments in this area.
The surgeon dissects and identifies the cut edge of the mesentery near the duodenum, then proceeds to transect the mesentery starting at the proximal transverse colon.
The surgeon proceeds distally towards the splenic flexure, using a ligature device to maintain hemostasis throughout the dissection.
The surgeon controls the final piece of mesentery, completing the laparoscopic dissection of the colon.
The surgeon begins the exteriorization of the specimen, preparing to remove the dissected colon segment from the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon places a small wound protector at the future ostomy site, preparing the area for subsequent surgical steps.
The surgeon secures the transected mesentery in place near the proximal transverse colon, completing the dissection process.
The surgeon carefully extracts the colon through the ostomy site, ensuring there is no spillage or contamination during the exteriorization process.
The surgeon fires a surgical stapler across the terminal ileum, securing the tissue with staples.
The specimen is removed from the surgical field following its exteriorization.
The video shows a classic example of ulcerative colitis, with severe inflammation visible in the distal colon while the proximal colon appears relatively spared.
The surgeon constructs the end ileostomy, ensuring the correct orientation of the bowel segment as confirmed laparoscopically.
The surgeon creates an ileostomy using the Brook technique, turning the end of the ileum back and suturing it to the skin, resulting in a smooth, rounded, inside-out ileum forming the stoma.
The video shows the completed ileostomy with the ostomy bag covering the stoma on the abdominal wall. A temporary drain is positioned to the side of the ostomy bag.
